group-b-strep-pregnancy.title: Group B Strep in Pregnancy: Testing, Risks, and What Happens During Labour
group-b-strep-pregnancy.description: What Group B Strep means for your pregnancy and birth — how common it is, how testing works in the UK and US, and how IV antibiotics in labour protect your baby.
group-b-strep-pregnancy.author: PregnancySprout Editorial Team
group-b-strep-pregnancy.content:
> **Fact-Checked & Transparent:** This article is fact-checked against current CDC, WHO, ACOG, and NHS guidelines. All health information is based on authoritative medical sources. Last verified: June 2026.

> ⚠️ **MEDICAL DISCLAIMER**
>
> This article is educational information only. It is NOT a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
>
> Always consult your pediatrician or healthcare provider before making any medical decisions for your baby or child. Every baby is unique, and professional medical guidance is essential.
>
> In case of emergency, call 911 or your local emergency number.

## What Is Group B Streptococcus?

Group B Streptococcus (GBS) — also called Streptococcus agalactiae or Group B Strep — is a type of bacteria found naturally in the gut and lower genital tract of many healthy adults. It is not a sexually transmitted infection and does not cause symptoms in the vast majority of people who carry it. Most adults who carry GBS have no idea.

Estimates suggest that between 20 and 30% of adults carry GBS at any given time, according to the Group B Strep Support charity (the UK's leading GBS charity). Colonisation is intermittent — a person may carry the bacteria one month and not the next, which has significant implications for testing accuracy.

During pregnancy, the concern is not about harm to the mother. GBS rarely causes problems for healthy adults. The concern is what can happen if GBS is present in the vagina or rectum at the time of birth, and the baby passes through an area where the bacteria are present.

## How GBS Is Transmitted to a Baby

Newborns are exposed to GBS during labour and delivery — through the birth canal, or in rare cases through the membranes before delivery. Most babies exposed to GBS do not become ill. The bacteria do not reliably cause infection in every newborn they come into contact with. However, in a small proportion of cases, GBS can cause a serious, rapidly progressing neonatal infection.

The risk of a baby being infected is higher in specific circumstances. Understanding these risk factors is central to the approach taken by UK and US healthcare systems.

## Risk Factors for Passing GBS to a Baby

The following risk factors significantly increase the likelihood of a baby developing GBS disease if exposed during birth:

- **Preterm labour** (before 37 weeks): Preterm babies are more vulnerable because their immune systems are less mature.
- **Prolonged rupture of membranes**: If membranes rupture more than 18 hours before delivery, the baby has more prolonged exposure.
- **Fever during labour**: A temperature of 38°C or above in labour is a risk factor for GBS and for infection generally.
- **A previous baby affected by GBS disease**: A history of a previous baby with GBS disease is one of the strongest risk factors.
- **GBS found in urine during this pregnancy**: GBS bacteriuria indicates heavy colonisation and is an automatic trigger for antibiotics in labour in both UK and US guidance.
- **Known GBS carrier status in this pregnancy** (for women who have been tested).

## The US Approach: Routine Testing at 35–37 Weeks

In the United States, the CDC and the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) recommend universal screening of all pregnant women for GBS between 35 and 37 weeks of pregnancy. The test involves a combined vaginal-rectal swab, which is sent to a laboratory.

If the swab comes back positive, the woman receives intravenous (IV) antibiotics during labour as a standard precaution, regardless of whether other risk factors are present.

The logic behind universal screening is straightforward: it identifies carriers in advance and allows a planned antibiotic protocol to be in place for labour. The limitation is that colonisation is intermittent — a woman who tests negative at 35 weeks may have acquired GBS by the time labour begins.

## The UK Approach: Risk-Based Testing

The NHS in the UK does not offer routine antenatal GBS screening to all pregnant women. This is a deliberate policy decision, not an oversight. The National Screening Committee reviewed the evidence and concluded that universal screening does not reliably predict which babies will be at risk, due to the fluctuating nature of colonisation, and could lead to very high numbers of women receiving unnecessary antibiotics in labour.

Instead, the UK uses a risk-factor-based approach. Women are offered IV antibiotics in labour if they have one or more of the clinical risk factors listed above. GBS found incidentally — for example on a swab taken for another reason, or via a private test — is treated as a risk factor and triggers antibiotic offer.

Some UK organisations, including the Group B Strep Support charity, argue that routine testing should be offered. This remains an area of ongoing debate within UK obstetrics.

## Private GBS Testing in the UK

Some women in the UK choose to have a private GBS swab test, available from organisations including the Group B Strep Support charity's testing programme. These are usually done at around 35–37 weeks. A positive result means a woman can inform her midwife and request IV antibiotics in labour.

It is worth knowing that a negative private swab result does not guarantee GBS-free status at the time of labour — colonisation can change. A negative result reduces the likelihood but does not eliminate risk. This should be part of the conversation when interpreting a negative test.

## Antibiotics in Labour: What to Expect

The treatment for known or suspected GBS carrier status in labour is intravenous antibiotics. The first-line antibiotic is penicillin (benzylpenicillin), given through a cannula (a small needle in a vein). This is highly effective at reducing the amount of bacteria present as the baby passes through the birth canal.

For women with a penicillin allergy, alternative antibiotics including clindamycin or cefazolin may be used, depending on allergy severity. It is important to inform your midwife of any antibiotic allergies at your booking appointment.

Antibiotics need to be started at least 4 hours before delivery to provide optimal protection. This means that for women who are GBS positive or have risk factors, going to hospital early when labour begins is strongly advised — you should not wait until contractions are very frequent before heading in.

The antibiotics do not guarantee that the baby will not be exposed to GBS, but they substantially reduce the risk of the baby developing infection. NHS and ACOG guidance both support this approach as effective.

## What GBS Disease Looks Like in Newborns

GBS infection in newborns is classified as either early-onset or late-onset.

### Early-Onset GBS Disease

Early-onset disease develops within the first 7 days of life, and most commonly in the first 12–24 hours. It is the most common form of GBS disease in newborns and is the type that intrapartum antibiotics target.

Symptoms in the newborn include:

- Breathing difficulties or grunting
- Floppiness or unusual limpness
- Fever or abnormally low temperature
- Difficulty feeding or poor feeding
- High-pitched or unusual crying
- Pale, mottled, or bluish skin
- Seizures (in more severe cases)

These symptoms can develop very rapidly. If you notice any of these in your newborn — especially in the first 24 hours — seek medical attention immediately. This is a medical emergency.

Early-onset GBS can cause septicaemia (blood infection), pneumonia, and meningitis. Treated promptly with IV antibiotics, many babies recover fully. Outcomes are significantly worse when diagnosis is delayed.

### Late-Onset GBS Disease

Late-onset GBS disease develops between 7 days and 3 months of life. It is less directly linked to labour and delivery and is not reliably prevented by intrapartum antibiotics. The source of infection in late-onset cases is often unclear — it may come from the environment, other people, or the mother's breast milk (though this is uncommon).

Symptoms are similar to early-onset disease. GBS meningitis is more commonly associated with late-onset disease and can cause long-term complications. Any newborn showing signs of serious illness in the first 3 months of life requires urgent medical assessment.

## Outcomes With and Without Treatment

The Group B Strep Support charity estimates that approximately 1 in 1,750 babies born in the UK develops GBS disease. With prompt treatment, most survive. However, GBS remains the most common cause of life-threatening infection in newborns in the UK.

Without treatment, the outcomes of GBS disease are substantially worse — a small number of affected babies die, and some survivors have lasting disabilities including deafness, vision impairment, or neurological difficulties following meningitis.

The administration of intrapartum antibiotics to women with risk factors has been shown to significantly reduce the rate of early-onset GBS disease in newborns.

## What GBS-Positive Women Should Do in Labour

If you are known to carry GBS, have had a previous baby with GBS disease, or have other risk factors, the following steps are important:

- Inform your midwife at the start of labour and at every handover of care.
- Go to the hospital or birth centre early when labour begins — do not delay, as antibiotics need at least 4 hours to work.
- Do not wait until contractions are 3–4 minutes apart if you carry GBS — get assessed sooner.
- If you have a home birth planned and carry GBS, discuss with your midwife how IV antibiotics will be administered and whether your home birth plan is still appropriate.
- After birth, make sure the neonatal team is informed of your GBS status so your baby can be monitored appropriately.

## Breastfeeding and GBS

GBS status does not affect your ability to breastfeed. The NHS does not advise against breastfeeding for GBS-positive mothers. While GBS can occasionally be detected in breast milk, transmission of GBS disease via breast milk is rare and breastfeeding is not considered a significant risk factor for late-onset GBS in most cases.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### If I tested negative for GBS at 35 weeks, does that mean my baby is safe?

A negative swab at 35–37 weeks significantly reduces — but does not eliminate — the risk of GBS being present at the time of labour. GBS colonisation can change between the time of testing and delivery. Women with a negative swab but who develop risk factors during labour (fever, prolonged rupture of membranes, preterm labour) may still be offered antibiotics based on those risk factors.

### Will I automatically be tested for GBS on the NHS?

Not unless you have risk factors or GBS is found incidentally on another swab or urine test. The NHS does not offer universal GBS screening. If you want a test, you can ask your midwife about the clinical risk-factor approach or access private testing. If GBS is found in any test during pregnancy, inform your midwife.

### What if I go into labour very quickly and there is no time for antibiotics?

If there is insufficient time for IV antibiotics (less than 4 hours before delivery), your baby will be monitored more closely after birth for signs of GBS disease. The paediatric team should be informed. Monitoring typically involves observation for at least 12 hours and may include blood tests depending on symptoms and risk factors.

### Can GBS cause problems for me as the pregnant woman?

In most cases, GBS colonisation causes no symptoms or problems for the pregnant woman. In rare cases, GBS can cause a urinary tract infection (UTI) in pregnancy, which is treated with oral antibiotics. If untreated, GBS UTI in pregnancy can contribute to preterm labour.

### Does GBS go away on its own without treatment?

GBS colonisation does fluctuate naturally and may not be detectable at delivery even if positive during pregnancy. However, this is not predictable, which is why women with known carrier status are offered intrapartum antibiotics regardless of when testing was done.

### Is GBS common in subsequent pregnancies if I had it before?

Women who were GBS-positive in a previous pregnancy have a higher likelihood of being positive in subsequent pregnancies, though this is not certain. Previous GBS carrier status is noted in your obstetric records and will be taken into account in future pregnancies.

## Key Takeaways

- GBS is carried by approximately 20–30% of adults with no symptoms; the concern in pregnancy is the risk of passing the bacteria to the newborn during birth.
- The US offers routine GBS swabs to all women at 35–37 weeks; the UK uses a risk-factor-based approach rather than universal screening.
- IV penicillin during labour is highly effective at reducing early-onset GBS disease in newborns and is the standard first-line treatment.
- Going to hospital early in labour is essential for GBS-positive women — antibiotics need at least 4 hours before delivery to work effectively.
- Early-onset GBS disease develops within the first 24 hours of life; symptoms including breathing difficulty, floppiness, and fever require immediate medical attention.
- A negative GBS swab reduces but does not eliminate risk; women with clinical risk factors during labour are still offered antibiotics regardless of a previous negative result.

heartburn-during-pregnancy-why-it-happen.description: Learn heartburn during pregnancy: why it happens and how to get reli.... Practical strategies and answers to common parent questions.

heartburn-during-pregnancy-why-it-happen.imageAlt: Pregnant woman sitting comfortably while drinking ginger tea to soothe heartburn

faqs.q: When does heartburn typically start during pregnancy?
faqs.a: Heartburn can begin as early as the first trimester due to hormonal changes, but it most commonly becomes noticeable during the second and third trimesters. As your baby grows and puts more pressure on your stomach, symptoms often intensify, with many women experiencing the worst heartburn between weeks 27 and 40.

faqs.q: Is it safe to take antacids while pregnant?
faqs.a: Many over-the-counter antacids containing calcium carbonate (like Tums) are generally considered safe during pregnancy and can provide quick relief. However, you should avoid antacids containing aluminum or high levels of sodium, and always consult your healthcare provider before starting any medication during pregnancy.

faqs.q: Does pregnancy heartburn mean my baby will have lots of hair?
faqs.a: Interestingly, a small 2006 study from Johns Hopkins University found a correlation between pregnancy heartburn severity and babies born with more hair. While the exact connection isn't fully understood, it's thought that the hormones relaxing the esophageal sphincter may also promote fetal hair growth. However, many factors influence both heartburn and baby's hair!

heartburn-during-pregnancy-why-it-happen.content:
If you've ever woken up in the middle of the night with a burning sensation creeping up your chest, or found yourself unable to enjoy your favourite meal without paying for it later, you're far from alone. Heartburn during pregnancy is one of the most common complaints expectant mothers face, affecting up to 80% of pregnant women at some point during their journey. That fiery feeling behind your breastbone might seem like just another pregnancy inconvenience, but understanding why it happens—and more importantly, how to find relief—can make a significant difference in your daily comfort and overall wellbeing.

The good news? While pregnancy heartburn can be uncomfortable and persistent, it's rarely harmful to you or your baby, and there are plenty of safe, effective strategies to help you manage it. Let's dive into everything you need to know about this common pregnancy symptom and how you can reclaim your comfort.

> **Fact-Checked & Transparent:** This article is fact-checked against current CDC, WHO, ACOG, and NHS guidelines. All health information is based on authoritative medical sources. Last verified: June 2026.

> ⚠️ **MEDICAL DISCLAIMER**
>
> This article is educational information only. It is NOT a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
>
> Always consult your pediatrician or healthcare provider before making any medical decisions for your baby or child. Every baby is unique, and professional medical guidance is essential.
>
> In case of emergency, call 911 or your local emergency number.

## What Is Heartburn and Why Does Pregnancy Make It Worse?

Heartburn, despite its name, has nothing to do with your heart. It's a digestive issue that occurs when stomach acid backs up into your oesophagus (the tube connecting your throat to your stomach), causing that characteristic burning sensation in your chest and throat. You might also experience a sour or bitter taste in your mouth, difficulty swallowing, or a feeling that food is stuck in your throat.

During pregnancy, several factors conspire to make heartburn more likely and more intense than you might have experienced before.

### The Hormone Factor

The primary culprit is progesterone, often called the "pregnancy hormone." This essential hormone does wonderful things for your pregnancy—it helps maintain the uterine lining and prevents premature contractions. However, progesterone also relaxes smooth muscle tissue throughout your body, including the lower oesophageal sphincter (LES).

The LES is a ring of muscle that acts like a one-way valve between your oesophagus and stomach. When it's relaxed by progesterone, it doesn't close as tightly as it normally would, allowing stomach acid to escape upward. This relaxation begins early in pregnancy, which is why some women experience heartburn even in their first trimester.

### The Growing Baby Factor

As your pregnancy progresses and your uterus expands to accommodate your growing baby, it begins to push against your stomach and other digestive organs. This physical pressure can force stomach contents—including acid—back up into your oesophagus. This is why many women find their heartburn intensifies during the third trimester when baby is taking up the most space.

If you're curious about how your baby is growing and developing week by week, our [Week by Week Guide](/pregnancy/week-by-week) can help you understand exactly what's happening at each stage of your pregnancy.

### Slower Digestion

Progesterone also slows down the digestive process overall, meaning food stays in your stomach longer. While this slower digestion allows more time for nutrients to be absorbed (beneficial for your growing baby), it also means your stomach produces acid for longer periods and remains fuller, increasing the likelihood of acid reflux.

## Common Triggers to Be Aware Of

While pregnancy hormones and your growing bump create the perfect conditions for heartburn, certain foods and habits can make symptoms worse. Being aware of these triggers can help you make choices that minimise discomfort.

### Foods That May Worsen Heartburn

Everyone's triggers are slightly different, but common culprits include:

- **Spicy foods** – Curries, hot peppers, and heavily spiced dishes
- **Citrus fruits and juices** – Oranges, lemons, grapefruits, and their juices
- **Tomatoes and tomato-based products** – Pasta sauces, ketchup, and salsa
- **Chocolate** – Sorry, but it can relax the LES further
- **Fatty or fried foods** – These slow digestion even more
- **Caffeine** – Coffee, tea, and caffeinated soft drinks
- **Carbonated beverages** – The bubbles can increase stomach pressure
- **Mint** – While it soothes some digestive issues, it can relax the LES
- **Garlic and onions** – Common triggers for many people

### Habits That Contribute to Heartburn

Beyond food choices, certain behaviours can increase your likelihood of experiencing heartburn:

- Eating large meals
- Eating too quickly
- Lying down soon after eating
- Wearing tight clothing around your waist
- Bending over after meals
- Eating close to bedtime

Keeping a simple food diary for a week or two can help you identify your personal triggers. Note what you eat, when you eat it, and when symptoms occur. This information can be invaluable in developing your own heartburn management strategy.

## Natural Remedies and Lifestyle Changes

Before reaching for medication, many women find significant relief through natural remedies and simple lifestyle modifications. These approaches are not only safe but can be surprisingly effective.

### Eating Habits That Help

**Eat smaller, more frequent meals.** Instead of three large meals, try eating five or six smaller ones throughout the day. This prevents your stomach from becoming too full and reduces pressure on the LES.

**Slow down.** Take your time eating, chew thoroughly, and put your fork down between bites. Eating slowly gives your body time to signal fullness and reduces the amount of air you swallow.

**Stay upright after eating.** Try to remain upright for at least two to three hours after meals. A gentle walk can aid digestion—just avoid bending over or doing activities that put pressure on your abdomen.

**Don't eat close to bedtime.** Aim to finish your last meal or snack at least three hours before lying down for the night.

### Sleep Positioning

How you sleep can make a significant difference in nighttime heartburn:

**Elevate the head of your bed.** Place blocks under the legs at the head of your bed to raise it by 15-20 centimetres (6-8 inches). This uses gravity to help keep stomach acid where it belongs. Simply using extra pillows isn't as effective and can cause neck strain.

**Sleep on your left side.** This position keeps your stomach below your oesophagus and can reduce acid reflux. It also happens to be the recommended sleeping position during pregnancy for optimal blood flow to your baby.

### Natural Soothers

Several natural remedies may help ease heartburn symptoms:

**Ginger** – Known for its digestive benefits, ginger can help soothe the stomach. Try ginger tea, ginger chews, or adding fresh ginger to your meals.

**Almonds** – Some women swear by eating a handful of almonds after meals. The theory is that their natural oils may help neutralise stomach acid.

**Milk or yoghurt** – A small glass of cold milk or a few spoonfuls of yoghurt can provide temporary relief by coating the stomach lining.

**Papaya** – Fresh papaya contains enzymes that aid digestion. However, avoid unripe papaya during pregnancy.

**Honey in warm water** – A tablespoon of honey mixed with warm water may help soothe the oesophagus.

## Safe Medications for Pregnancy Heartburn

When lifestyle changes aren't enough, certain medications can provide relief. Always consult your healthcare provider before taking any medication during pregnancy, but here's an overview of what's generally considered safe.

### Antacids

Over-the-counter antacids like Tums, Rolaids, and Gaviscon work by neutralising stomach acid and can provide quick relief. Those containing calcium carbonate have the added benefit of providing extra calcium, which you need more of during pregnancy.

**What to avoid:** Antacids containing aluminium (which can cause constipation and, in high amounts, may affect fetal bone development), those high in sodium (which can increase water retention), and products containing aspirin.

### H2 Blockers

Medications like ranitidine and famotidine reduce the amount of acid your stomach produces. They take longer to work than antacids but provide longer-lasting relief. These are generally considered safe during pregnancy but should be used under medical guidance.

### Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs)

Medications like omeprazole work by blocking acid production more completely than H2 blockers. While they're often used for severe heartburn, their safety during pregnancy is still being studied. Your healthcare provider may recommend them if other options aren't effective.

If you're experiencing heartburn alongside other symptoms and aren't sure whether they're normal pregnancy discomforts, our [Symptom Checker](/tools/symptom-checker) can help you understand what you're experiencing and when to seek medical advice.

## When to Seek Medical Advice

While heartburn during pregnancy is usually harmless, there are times when you should contact your healthcare provider:

- **Severe symptoms** that don't respond to dietary changes or over-the-counter remedies
- **Difficulty swallowing** or feeling like food is getting stuck
- **Unexplained weight loss** due to inability to eat
- **Vomiting blood** or material that looks like coffee grounds
- **Black, tarry stools** which could indicate bleeding
- **Chest pain** that feels different from typical heartburn, especially if accompanied by shortness of breath, sweating, or pain radiating to your arm or jaw
- **Symptoms that significantly impact your quality of life** or ability to sleep

Your doctor or midwife can assess whether your symptoms require additional investigation or treatment and ensure there's no underlying condition that needs attention.

## Heartburn Through Each Trimester

Understanding how heartburn typically evolves throughout pregnancy can help you anticipate and manage symptoms more effectively.

### First Trimester

Heartburn can begin early, sometimes even before you know you're pregnant, due to rising progesterone levels. It may accompany morning sickness, making mealtimes particularly challenging. The silver lining? The combination often improves as the first trimester ends.

### Second Trimester

For many women, the second trimester brings some relief from early pregnancy symptoms, including heartburn. However, as your baby grows and your uterus expands, you may notice symptoms creeping back, particularly after large meals or certain trigger foods.

### Third Trimester

This is typically when heartburn is at its worst. Your growing baby is pushing against your stomach, leaving less room for food and increasing pressure that can force acid upward. The good news? Many women experience significant relief soon after delivery when hormone levels normalise and baby is no longer pressing on their stomach.

As you prepare for your baby's arrival during these final weeks, our [Registry Checklist](/tools/registry-checklist) can help ensure you have everything ready for your little one.

## The Emotional Side of Constant Discomfort

Living with persistent heartburn can be exhausting and frustrating. It's not just a physical symptom—it can affect your sleep, your enjoyment of food, your social life, and your overall mood. If you're feeling worn down by constant discomfort, know that these feelings are completely valid.

Don't hesitate to:

- **Talk to your partner or support system** about how you're feeling
- **Reach out to your healthcare provider** if symptoms are significantly impacting your quality of life
- **Connect with other pregnant women** through antenatal classes or online communities who understand what you're going through
- **Practice self-compassion** – pregnancy is hard work, and it's okay to acknowledge that

Remember that this discomfort is temporary. For most women, heartburn resolves quickly after delivery, and soon you'll be focused on the incredible joy of your new baby rather than the challenges of pregnancy.

## Looking Ahead: Relief Is Coming

Pregnancy heartburn, while uncomfortable and sometimes seemingly endless, is a temporary condition that will improve. For most women, symptoms begin to ease soon after giving birth as hormone levels return to normal and your organs have room to settle back into their usual positions.

In the meantime, be patient with yourself and experiment with different strategies to find what works best for you. Whether it's avoiding your trigger foods, eating smaller meals, sleeping propped up, or using safe medications when needed, there are plenty of ways to manage this common pregnancy complaint.

As you navigate heartburn and all the other changes pregnancy brings, remember that each day brings you closer to meeting your little one. The discomforts you're experiencing now are part of the incredible process your body is going through to grow and nurture new life—and that's truly something remarkable.

If you haven't already, check out our [Due Date Calculator](/tools/due-date-calculator) to see exactly how many weeks are left until you can expect to meet your baby and say goodbye to pregnancy heartburn for good. You've got this, and we're here to support you every step of the way.

hospital-bag-checklist.title: Hospital Bag Checklist 2026
hospital-bag-checklist.description: Learn hospital bag checklist 2026: everything you need for birth dur.... Practical strategies and answers to common parent questions.
hospital-bag-checklist.author: PregnancySprout Editorial Team
hospital-bag-checklist.imageAlt: Hospital bag packed and ready for labor and delivery

faqs.q: When should I pack my hospital bag?
faqs.a: Pack your hospital bag by week 36 of pregnancy. If you're expecting multiples or have any pregnancy complications, aim for week 32–34. You don't want to be searching for your phone charger during early labour.

faqs.q: How many outfits should I bring for baby?
faqs.a: Pack 2–3 newborn outfits and 2–3 0-3 month outfits. Babies are unpredictable in size, so having both sizes ensures you're covered. Include a going-home outfit that's easy to put on.

faqs.q: Will the hospital provide nappies and formula?
faqs.a: Most hospitals provide basic nappies, formula (if not breastfeeding), and basic toiletries. However, supplies vary by hospital — always call ahead to ask what's provided to avoid overpacking.

hospital-bag-checklist.content:
Packing your hospital bag is one of those third-trimester milestones that feels equal parts exciting and overwhelming. Pack too little and you'll be sending someone out for supplies mid-labour. Pack too much and you'll be dragging two suitcases through the maternity ward.

This guide covers exactly what experienced mothers (and their birth partners) actually use — stripped of everything that sounds nice in theory but sits untouched in the bag.

> **Fact-Checked & Transparent:** This article is fact-checked against current CDC, WHO, ACOG, and NHS guidelines. All health information is based on authoritative medical sources. Last verified: June 2026.

> ⚠️ **MEDICAL DISCLAIMER**
>
> This article is educational information only. It is NOT a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
>
> Always consult your pediatrician or healthcare provider before making any medical decisions for your baby or child. Every baby is unique, and professional medical guidance is essential.
>
> In case of emergency, call 911 or your local emergency number.

## When to Pack

**Pack by week 36.** If you're carrying multiples, or have any pregnancy complications, pack by week 32–34. Labour has a way of arriving unexpectedly, and the last thing you want during early contractions is to be frantically searching for your insurance card.

Pro tip: keep a list on your phone of last-minute items to grab (phone charger, snacks, birth plan) so you don't need to pack them weeks in advance.

## For Labour: What You'll Actually Use

### Comfort Items
- **Your own pillow** — hospital pillows are notoriously flat; bring one with a colourful case so it doesn't get mixed up
- **Warm, non-slip socks** — hospital floors are cold and you'll be on your feet
- **Lip balm** — you'll use this more than almost anything else; labour causes dry lips
- **Hair ties** — multiple; they disappear
- **Cooling face mist or small fan** — labour is hot work
- **Massage tools or tennis balls** — for back pain relief (ask your partner to do this)

### Practical Items
- **Birth plan copies** (at least 3 printed copies) — see our guide on [how to write a birth plan](/blog/how-to-write-a-birth-plan-templates-and-tips)
- **Insurance cards and ID**
- **Phone and charger** — a long cable is useful
- **Camera or memory card** — or just use your phone
- **Headphones** — your own music/podcast during early labour
- **Snacks** — more on this below

### Snacks: Don't Underestimate This
Labour can last many hours. Even if you can't eat during active labour, early labour is long and you'll be hungry. Your birth partner will definitely be hungry. Pack:
- Cereal bars, trail mix, and crackers
- Isotonic sports drinks (for energy and hydration during labour)
- Lollipops — surprisingly helpful during active labour for giving your mouth something to do
- Individual snack packs that are easy to eat one-handed

## For After Birth: Mum's Hospital Stay

### Clothing
- **2–3 nightgowns or pyjamas** — front-opening if breastfeeding; button-front is ideal
- **Robe** — for walking the corridors
- **Nursing bras** (2–3) — soft cup, wire-free; bring these even if you're undecided on feeding, as your milk will come in regardless
- **Comfortable underwear** (5+ pairs of disposable or very old pairs) — you'll be wearing maternity pads for days
- **Going-home outfit** — something loose; you'll still look 5–6 months pregnant immediately after birth

### Toiletries
- Shampoo, conditioner, and body wash (travel size)
- Face wash and moisturiser
- Dry shampoo (a post-birth hero product)
- Toothbrush and toothpaste
- Deodorant
- Gentle makeup if desired — for photos

### Post-Birth Essentials
- **Maternity pads** (the thickest you can find — bring at least 20) — the hospital provides some but not enough
- **Witch hazel pads** (Tucks) or perineal spray — for soreness after a vaginal birth
- **Nipple cream** (lanolin-based) — start applying before you even need it; your nipples will thank you from day one
- **Nipple pads** (breast pads) — your milk may come in on day 2–4 and leaking is very common even before that
- **Stool softener** — ask your provider about this; the first post-birth bowel movement is notoriously anxiety-inducing

## What to Pack for a Caesarean

If you know in advance you're having a planned caesarean, or if you have any reason to think an emergency caesarean might be possible, pack specifically for it. The standard list above applies, but these additions make a real difference to your recovery.

### Clothing for After a C-Section
- **Loose, high-waisted trousers or skirts** — anything that sits above the scar line. Low-waisted jeans or trousers that press directly on your incision will be genuinely painful for several weeks. Maternity trousers with a soft waistband often work well post-surgery.
- **Knickers that sit above the scar** — standard post-surgery advice from NHS maternity units. High-leg cotton knickers rather than bikini-cut styles reduce irritation significantly.
- **No tight waistbands at all** for the first 2–4 weeks — skirts and dresses are often the most comfortable option in the early weeks.

### For the Journey Home
- **A firm pillow to hold against your abdomen** — holding a pillow over the incision site when you cough, sneeze, laugh, or go over a bump in the car is standard advice given by NHS midwives. It provides counter-pressure and dramatically reduces the sharp pulling sensation. Pack this in the bag, not the boot of the car.

### Digestive Comfort After a C-Section
- **Peppermint oil capsules** — trapped wind after abdominal surgery is one of the most uncomfortable and undertalked aspects of C-section recovery. Gas moves upwards and can cause significant shoulder and chest pain in the first 24–48 hours. Peppermint oil capsules or peppermint tea help to dispel this. Many hospitals do not routinely offer these, so bring your own.
- **Mild laxative** — ask your midwife or GP before discharge about an appropriate gentle laxative. After abdominal surgery, bowel function slows considerably. The combination of anaesthetic, pain medication (particularly codeine), reduced mobility, and anxiety about straining makes constipation almost universal. Your care team may prescribe something, but it is worth knowing to ask.

### Nursing Bra Specifics for C-Section Recovery
Standard nursing bra guidance applies — wire-free, soft cup — but with one additional criterion: you need a bra you can open **entirely one-handed**. After a C-section, getting your other arm into a position to manipulate clips or fasteners may be uncomfortable or impossible in the early days. Front-clasp nursing bras or simple crossover styles are the most practical. Practise opening yours before you go into hospital.

## For Baby

### Clothing
- **2–3 newborn (NB) babygrows/sleepsuits** — with built-in scratch mitts if possible
- **2–3 0-3 month babygrows** — newborn size sometimes only fits for days
- **Going-home outfit** — something that goes over the head easily or has a wide neck
- **Hat** (2) — newborns lose heat rapidly through their heads; the hospital may provide one but bring your own too
- **Muslin cloths** (4–6) — these are multipurpose: swaddle, burp cloth, sun shade

### Feeding Supplies
- **Breast pump** (if using) — particularly useful for initial milk establishment
- **Ready-made formula** (if formula feeding) — 4–6 individual ready-to-feed bottles; far easier than making up feeds in hospital
- **2–4 bottles** with slow-flow teats
- **Sterilising tablets** — for on-the-go sterilising in the sink

### Nappy Changing
- **Nappies** (1 small pack of newborn size) — the hospital provides some but packs run out
- **Nappy cream** — Bepanthen or similar
- **Unscented baby wipes** or cotton wool pads with water

### Going Home
- **Car seat** — properly fitted and installed before birth; without this, you cannot legally leave the hospital with your baby
- **Baby blanket** — for warmth on the way home

## For Your Birth Partner

Don't forget the person who'll be with you for potentially 24+ hours:

- Change of clothes
- Toothbrush and basic toiletries
- Phone and charger
- Snacks and drinks
- Cash for the vending machine and car park
- Something to do during slow periods (book, tablet, downloaded Netflix)
- Comfortable shoes — they'll be on their feet too

## What to Leave Home

- Expensive jewellery or valuables
- Heavy books (you won't read them)
- Too many fancy clothes for baby (they'll be in a nappy and babygrow)
- Elaborate birth plan portfolios — one page, three copies is plenty
- Your entire skincare routine

## Packing Your Bag: Practical Tips

1. **Use separate bags** — one for labour, one for post-birth, one for baby. Clearly labelled bags save enormous frustration when your birth partner is searching for nappies at 3am.

2. **Roll clothes** rather than folding — saves space significantly.

3. **Keep it in your car from week 36** — so you're never caught unprepared.

4. **Tell your birth partner where everything is** — they need to be able to find things quickly.

Use our complete [Baby Registry Checklist](/tools/registry-checklist) to make sure you have everything you need set up at home before your baby arrives.

## The Most Important Thing

All of this is preparation — but the hospital you're going to has delivered thousands of babies. They have everything you truly need. The bag exists for your comfort, not your survival.

Pack it, tick it off your list, and let yourself focus on the incredible thing that's about to happen.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Do I need a separate bag for a planned C-section?**
Not necessarily a separate bag, but you do need specific items added to the standard list. The most important additions are: high-waisted, loose trousers or skirts that don't press on the scar; a firm pillow for the car journey home; peppermint oil capsules for trapped wind; a gentle laxative; and a nursing bra you can operate one-handed. Prepare these before your admission date.

**How many maternity pads do I actually need in hospital?**
Bring at least 20 for a 1–2 night stay. Postpartum bleeding (lochia) is heaviest in the first 24–72 hours and you may go through several pads per hour initially. The hospital provides some, but not enough. NHS guidance recommends having plenty available — it is one of the most commonly underestimated items.

**Can I eat and drink during labour?**
NHS guidance allows eating and drinking during low-risk labour. During early labour especially, staying hydrated and having light carbohydrates (toast, crackers, fruit) maintains your energy. Many hospitals restrict eating once an epidural is in place or if a caesarean becomes likely, so follow your care team's specific advice on the day.

**What if I go into labour before my bag is packed?**
Send your birth partner to grab the phone charger, ID and insurance card, and your birth plan. Everything else can be brought later or borrowed from the hospital. The essentials are: something to wear, ID, and the car seat for getting home.

**Do I need to pack a going-home outfit for myself?**
Yes, and plan it in advance. Many women underestimate how they'll look and feel in the days immediately after birth. Your body will still look approximately 5–6 months pregnant. Pack something loose, comfortable, and easy to manage — a loose dress or maternity trousers with a soft top works well. For C-section recovery specifically, make sure nothing sits at your incision line.

**Should I bring my own snacks even if the hospital has a cafeteria?**
Yes. Labour is unpredictable in timing, and the cafeteria may not be open when you need it. Your birth partner will be with you continuously and also needs sustenance. Pack enough for both of you for at least 24 hours — this is one of the most universally praised additions to any hospital bag list.

## Key Takeaways

- Pack your hospital bag by week 36; by week 32–34 if carrying multiples or if complications are present
- For a planned or potential caesarean, add high-waisted loose trousers, a pillow for the car journey home, peppermint oil capsules for trapped wind, a mild laxative, and a one-handed nursing bra
- Nipple cream and nipple pads belong in the bag from the start — apply nipple cream before feeding begins, not after soreness sets in
- Maternity pads are almost always underpacked; bring at least 20 for a short stay
- Use separate labelled bags for labour, post-birth, and baby — it saves significant time when your birth partner is searching at 3am
- The car seat must be fitted and installed before labour begins; no hospital will allow you to leave without one for your baby

> 📋 **SOURCES & FACT-CHECKING**
>
> This article is compiled and verified against these authoritative sources:
> - [CDC](https://cdc.gov) (Centers for Disease Control & Prevention)
> - [WHO](https://who.int) (World Health Organization)
> - [ACOG](https://acog.org) (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists)
> - [NHS](https://nhs.uk) (National Health Service)
> - [AAP](https://aap.org) (American Academy of Pediatrics)
>
> Last verified: June 2026
>
> **Educational content only.** Always consult your pediatrician for medical decisions.

how-often-bathe-newborn.title: How Often Should You Bathe a Newborn? NHS Guidance Explained
how-often-bathe-newborn.description: How often to bathe a newborn, when to start, what temperature, how long, and how to keep the umbilical cord stump clean until it falls off.
how-often-bathe-newborn.author: PregnancySprout Editorial Team
how-often-bathe-newborn.content:
# How Often Should You Bathe a Newborn? NHS Guidance Explained

Most new parents assume that daily bathing is part of newborn care. In fact, the NHS recommends bathing a newborn two to three times per week — not every day. Understanding why, and knowing what to do on the days between baths, removes a common source of anxiety and protects the baby's skin in the process.

> **Fact-Checked & Transparent:** This article is fact-checked against current CDC, WHO, ACOG, and NHS guidelines. All health information is based on authoritative medical sources. Last verified: June 2026.

> ⚠️ **MEDICAL DISCLAIMER**
>
> This article is educational information only. It is NOT a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
>
> Always consult your pediatrician or healthcare provider before making any medical decisions for your baby or child. Every baby is unique, and professional medical guidance is essential.
>
> In case of emergency, call 911 or your local emergency number.

## Why Not Daily

Newborn skin is not a smaller version of adult skin. It lacks the fully developed acid mantle — the slightly acidic surface film that acts as a barrier against pathogens — and the microbiome is still establishing itself in the weeks after birth. Over-washing disrupts both. Frequent bathing strips the skin of natural oils, increases transepidermal water loss, and can contribute to dryness, sensitivity, and irritation. For the first few weeks, less is genuinely more.

The NHS is explicit on this point. Two to three baths per week is sufficient to keep a newborn clean; more frequent bathing offers no hygiene benefit and carries a real skin cost.

## The Umbilical Cord Stump

The most important bathing rule in the first weeks of life is this: do not submerge a baby who still has their umbilical cord stump. The stump — the dried remnant of the umbilical cord — typically falls off between one and three weeks after birth, though some take longer. Until it does, the baby should receive sponge baths only.

Keep the stump dry. Exposure to water slows the drying process that leads to natural separation, and a wet stump is more vulnerable to infection. Fold nappies down away from the stump to allow air circulation, and avoid covering it with clothing that sits tightly against it.

The stump will change colour as it dries — from yellow-green to brown to black — and this is normal. Never attempt to pull or twist it off, even when it appears to be barely attached. It will separate on its own.

Signs of infection that require immediate GP review include: redness that spreads onto the skin of the abdomen (not just around the stump itself), any discharge with an unpleasant smell, swelling at the base of the stump, and fever in the baby. A small amount of dried blood at the point of separation is normal; pus or spreading redness is not.

## Top and Tailing

On the days between baths, top and tailing keeps a newborn clean without a full immersion. Use warm water, cotton wool balls or a soft cloth, and no soap. Clean the face first — eyes wiped gently from the inner corner outward with a fresh piece of cotton wool for each eye, then the rest of the face, behind the ears, and under the chin. Move to the neck folds (a common site for milk to accumulate), the armpits, and finally the nappy area, using clean cotton wool for each new area. The NHS recommends plain water for top and tailing in the first weeks; soap is unnecessary and can be drying.

## Bath Temperature

The correct bath water temperature for a newborn is 37 to 38 degrees Celsius — body temperature, or just above. Use a bath thermometer if one is available. In the absence of a thermometer, test the water with the inside of your wrist or your elbow — areas more sensitive to temperature than the back of the hand. The water should feel warm but not hot.

One safety rule that is absolute: never add hot water to a bath while the baby is in it. Fill the bath before placing the baby in, and check the temperature before immersion. A baby cannot pull away from scalding water the way an older child or adult can.

## Bath Length

Newborn baths should last no more than 5 to 10 minutes. Babies lose heat quickly in water, and extended bathing serves no purpose at this age. Have a towel ready to wrap the baby immediately when they come out, paying particular attention to drying the neck folds, behind the ears, and the nappy area, as moisture trapped in skin folds can cause irritation.

## Never Leave a Baby Unattended in Water

This rule has no exceptions. A newborn can drown in a few centimetres of water in seconds. If you need to leave the room — if the phone rings, if another child needs attention — take the baby with you. Do not rely on bath seats, infant bathtubs, or any other support device as a substitute for supervision. These products keep a baby positioned; they do not keep a baby safe if unattended.

## Products to Use (and Avoid)

For the first several weeks, plain water is all that is needed for bathing and top and tailing. If a cleanser is used, the NHS recommends a very mild, unperfumed baby wash in small amounts. Bubble bath, heavily scented products, and adult soaps should be avoided entirely in the newborn period — they are too harsh for immature skin and offer no benefit. Even products marketed as "gentle" or "natural" may contain fragrances or preservatives that irritate newborn skin.

## When to Start Regular Baths

Once the umbilical cord stump has fallen off and the area is fully healed, a baby can be placed in a shallow bath (approximately 8 to 10 centimetres of water). Continue bathing two to three times per week unless there is a specific reason for more frequent washing — after a particularly messy feed, for example. The NHS guidance does not change significantly after the stump heals; daily bathing remains unnecessary for most healthy newborns.

## The Newborn Skin Microbiome

The newborn skin microbiome — the community of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms that live on the skin surface — begins establishing itself immediately after birth and continues to develop throughout the first months of life. This microbiome is not a contamination problem; it is a protective system that plays an active role in immune development and skin barrier function.

Vernix caseosa — the white, waxy coating that covers a newborn at birth — is often immediately wiped away in hospital settings, but research supports leaving it in place for as long as possible. Vernix has antimicrobial properties, reduces transepidermal water loss, and helps seed the skin microbiome. The WHO recommends delaying the first bath for at least six hours after birth, and ideally 24 hours, specifically to preserve vernix and its benefits for skin and immune development.

Frequent bathing in the first weeks interferes with microbiome establishment. Soap and water — even mild baby soap — reduce microbial diversity on the skin. For most healthy newborns, twice-weekly bathing is compatible with healthy microbiome development; daily bathing is not. This is particularly relevant for babies with a family history of eczema, where early disruption of the skin microbiome is associated with higher rates of eczema onset. The NHS recommends avoiding soap and fragranced products for babies at increased eczema risk, and this guidance aligns with what is known about protecting the developing microbiome.

## The Evening Bath as a Sleep Cue

Bath time can do more than clean — when incorporated consistently into a bedtime routine, it becomes a powerful sleep cue that many parents find genuinely useful.

The mechanism is physiological. Core body temperature drops naturally in the hour before sleep, signalling to the brain that it is time to wind down. A warm bath raises core temperature temporarily; when the baby comes out of the bath and the body begins to cool, that cooling process mimics and amplifies the natural pre-sleep temperature drop. The result is that many babies become noticeably drowsy after bath time.

This effect is well documented in adults and increasingly recognised in infants. Research on bedtime routines consistently shows that babies with predictable, consistent pre-sleep routines — bath, feed, sleep — settle more easily and sleep for longer stretches than those without routines. The NHS and AAP both recommend consistent bedtime routines from around six to eight weeks as part of healthy sleep hygiene.

For the bath to function as a reliable sleep cue, it needs to be consistent — the same time each evening, in the same sequence (bath, then nappy and pyjamas, then feed, then sleep). After a few weeks of repetition, the bath itself becomes a signal. Babies begin to show drowsy cues during or immediately after bath time because they have learned to associate it with sleep.

Not every baby responds the same way. Some find baths stimulating rather than calming, particularly in the first few weeks. If this is the case, moving bath time to earlier in the evening — an hour before the desired sleep time rather than immediately before — can preserve the temperature-drop effect while allowing more time for active arousal to settle.

## Managing Dry Skin Between Baths

Dry, flaking skin is extremely common in newborns, particularly in the first two to three weeks. This is a normal process: the vernix that protected skin in utero begins to shed after birth, and the outer layers of skin adjust to their new environment. In most healthy newborns, peeling skin in the first weeks requires no treatment and resolves on its own.

If dryness persists past the first three weeks, or if the skin looks cracked, red, or inflamed, a plain emollient applied after bathing and between baths can help. The NHS recommends plain, fragrance-free emollient cream for babies with dry skin and as prophylaxis for babies at risk of eczema. Ingredients to avoid in baby skincare products include fragrance (listed as "parfum"), sodium lauryl sulphate (SLS) — a foaming agent that is known to irritate and dry skin — and unnecessary additives such as alcohol, parabens, and artificial colourants.

Products do not need to be expensive. Plain white soft paraffin (petroleum jelly/Vaseline) is one of the most effective emollients for dry newborn skin and is free of additives. Doublebase Gel and Diprobase Cream (available in UK pharmacies) are also fragrance-free and widely used on recommendation by NHS health visitors. Apply emollient immediately after bathing, while the skin is still slightly damp, to lock in moisture.

Avoid massage oils that contain nut-based ingredients — particularly peanut or almond oil — until allergy risk has been assessed. The NHS recommends caution with nut-derived oils in the first year given the association with nut allergy sensitisation.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**At what age can I stop sterilising bath equipment?**
Standard bath equipment — baby baths, support seats, bath toys — does not require sterilisation. Rinse thoroughly after each use and allow to dry fully to prevent mould. The sterilisation guidance from the NHS applies specifically to feeding equipment (bottles, teats, breast pump parts) until 12 months.

**My newborn hates bath time. What can I do?**
Newborn bath discomfort is usually related to the transition from warm water to cool air — have a warm towel ready to wrap them immediately. Ensure the room temperature is warm before undressing, use a hand to support the head securely throughout, and try bathing at a different time of day to find when they are most content. Some newborns take several weeks to become comfortable with bathing.

**Can I bathe my newborn every day if they enjoy it?**
Daily bathing is not recommended by the NHS or AAP in the first few months because of the impact on the developing skin barrier and microbiome. If your baby enjoys water, short periods of warm water play — holding their feet or hands in warm water — can provide sensory stimulation without the full effects of daily bathing.

**My baby's skin is peeling. Should I apply cream?**
Newborn skin peeling in the first two to three weeks is normal and does not require treatment. After this period, if dryness persists, a plain fragrance-free emollient is appropriate. See a GP if the skin looks cracked, weeping, or inflamed, as this may indicate eczema or another skin condition requiring assessment.

**How do I wash a newborn's hair without getting water in their eyes?**
Use the "football hold" — lay the baby along your forearm with their body tucked under your arm and their head supported over the bath. This positions the head so that water runs away from the face when you rinse. A small amount of plain water is sufficient; shampoo is not necessary in the first few weeks.

**Is it safe to bathe a newborn alone, without a second person?**
Yes, it is safe with the right preparation. Gather everything — towel, clean nappy, change of clothes, moisturiser — before running the bath. Never leave to retrieve forgotten items once the baby is in or near water. Ensure the phone is out of reach or silenced so you are not tempted to leave the room.

## Key Takeaways

- The NHS recommends bathing a newborn two to three times per week — not every day; daily bathing strips natural oils and disrupts the developing skin microbiome.
- Do not submerge a baby until the umbilical cord stump has fully separated and the area has healed; sponge baths and top-and-tailing are appropriate until then.
- Vernix — the white coating present at birth — has antimicrobial and moisturising properties; the WHO recommends delaying the first bath for at least six hours and ideally 24 hours after birth.
- A consistent evening bath, followed by feed and sleep, becomes an effective sleep cue because the post-bath cooling of core body temperature mimics the physiological preparation for sleep.
- For dry skin between baths, use a plain fragrance-free emollient; avoid products containing parfum, SLS, and nut-based oils.
- Never leave a baby unattended in or near water, even for a few seconds — drowning can occur in a small amount of water very quickly.

> 📋 **SOURCES & FACT-CHECKING**
>
> This article is compiled and verified against these authoritative sources:
> - [CDC](https://cdc.gov) (Centers for Disease Control & Prevention)
> - [WHO](https://who.int) (World Health Organization)
> - [ACOG](https://acog.org) (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists)
> - [NHS](https://nhs.uk) (National Health Service)
> - [AAP](https://aap.org) (American Academy of Pediatrics)
>
> Last verified: June 2026
>
> **Educational content only.** Always consult your pediatrician for medical decisions.

how-to-bathe-newborn-safely.title: How to Bathe a Newborn Safely: Temperature, Technique, and What to Avoid
how-to-bathe-newborn-safely.description: Step-by-step guide to bathing a newborn safely — the right water temperature, how to hold them, when to start baths, what products to use, and the safety rules that cannot be skipped.
how-to-bathe-newborn-safely.author: PregnancySprout Editorial Team
how-to-bathe-newborn-safely.content:
# How to Bathe a Newborn Safely: Temperature, Technique, and What to Avoid

Bathing a newborn for the first time is one of those moments that feels simultaneously special and terrifying. A tiny, slippery baby, water at just the right temperature, and the nagging worry that you might be doing something wrong. The good news is that newborn bathing is straightforward once you understand the basic rules — and there are only a few rules that truly matter.

This guide covers everything from when to start proper baths, how to hold your baby safely, the correct water temperature, which products to use, and the non-negotiable safety rules that NHS guidance consistently emphasises.

> **Fact-Checked & Transparent:** This article is fact-checked against current CDC, WHO, ACOG, and NHS guidelines. All health information is based on authoritative medical sources. Last verified: June 2026.

> ⚠️ **MEDICAL DISCLAIMER**
>
> This article is educational information only. It is NOT a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment.
>
> Always consult your pediatrician or healthcare provider before making any medical decisions for your baby or child. Every baby is unique, and professional medical guidance is essential.
>
> In case of emergency, call 911 or your local emergency number.

## When to Start Bathing a Newborn

For the first week or two after birth, a full immersion bath is not recommended. The NHS advises waiting until the umbilical cord stump has dried, shrivelled, and fallen off before placing your baby in water. This usually happens between one and three weeks after birth.

Until the stump falls off, top-and-tailing is the recommended approach. This means washing your baby's face, neck, hands, and nappy area each day using cotton wool and warm water, without submerging them in a bath. It keeps your baby clean without risking infection or prolonged moisture around the umbilical stump.

Once the stump has gone and the area has healed, you can introduce a proper bath. There is no rule that says you must bathe your baby daily — in fact, bathing two to three times per week is entirely sufficient for a newborn. Daily baths can strip the natural oils from newborn skin, which is already adjusting to life outside the womb. On non-bath days, continue with top-and-tailing for the face, neck folds, armpits, and nappy area.

## How to Prepare the Bath

Preparation matters more with a newborn than with any other stage of babyhood. Once your baby is undressed and in your arms, you cannot leave them to fetch something you forgot. Gather everything before you start.

You will need:

- A baby bath tub, or a large plastic tub placed in the main bath
- A non-slip mat if using the full-size bath
- Two or three soft cloths or cotton wool pads
- A pre-warmed hooded towel laid out flat on a safe surface nearby
- Clean nappy, barrier cream, and clothes ready
- Mild, fragrance-free baby wash if you are using any

Position the baby bath on a stable surface at waist height if possible, or place it in the bottom of the empty main bath. Kneeling beside it reduces back strain and gives you full control.

### Getting the Water Temperature Right

The NHS recommends a bath water temperature of 37 to 38 degrees Celsius — this is close to body temperature and will feel warm but not hot when you test it. The correct way to test the water is with your elbow or the inside of your wrist, not your hand. The skin on your hand is much less sensitive to heat than the skin on these areas.

Fill the bath to a depth of approximately 5 to 8 centimetres. This is shallow enough to keep your baby safe while still allowing you to wash them effectively.

Critically: never add hot water while the baby is already in the bath. Hot water poured into a bath that contains an infant can cause scalding even if you intend to mix it in. If the water has cooled and you need to top it up, lift the baby out, add the hot water, re-test the temperature thoroughly, and then return the baby to the bath.

The room itself should be warm — around 20 to 22 degrees Celsius — so your baby does not become cold when they leave the water.

## How to Hold Your Baby in the Bath

Holding a wet, soapy newborn safely is a skill that takes a few baths to feel natural. The most secure and recommended grip is the cross-cradle hold.

With your dominant hand, support your baby's bottom and thighs. With your non-dominant arm, slide it beneath their back so that your hand cups the back of their head and neck firmly. Your forearm runs along their spine. This position keeps their head well above the water at all times.

To lower your baby into the bath, support them in this hold and lower them feet-first. Feet going in first is gentler and less startling than lowering them back-first, and it allows you to feel the water temperature on their feet before the rest of their body is submerged.

Your non-dominant arm stays under their head throughout the entire bath. You wash with your free hand only.

## The Washing Sequence

### Face First

Begin with the face using plain warm water — no soap. Use a separate piece of damp cotton wool for each eye, wiping from the inner corner outward. This prevents cross-contamination between eyes. Use a fresh piece of cotton wool for the rest of the face, working around the nose, cheeks, and chin.

Pay attention to the neck folds and chin area where milk residue accumulates. Gently turn the head to access the folds but keep the rest of your arm firm and secure.

### Body Next

Wash the body, including the armpits, chest, tummy, legs, and between the toes. If you are using baby wash, a very small amount is all that is needed. The NHS advises using plain water for the first month of life to protect the skin's developing barrier. After one month, a mild, fragrance-free wash is fine if you choose to use one.

Clean the nappy area last. For girls, always wipe front to back. For boys, simply wash around the genitals without retracting the foreskin — it is not designed to be pulled back at this age.

### Hair Last

Not every bath needs to include hair washing. Washing your newborn's hair two to three times per week is sufficient.

When you do wash hair, tilt your baby's head gently backward over the water. This directs water away from the face when you rinse. Pour warm water over the scalp with your cupped hand or a small cup, and rinse from the back of the head toward the forehead — never the other way, as water running over the face distresses most newborns and can cause them to jerk suddenly.

Use a tiny amount of mild baby shampoo if desired, and rinse thoroughly.

## Getting Baby Out of the Bath

Lift your baby out using the same cross-cradle hold used to lower them in. Have the pre-warmed towel on a firm, flat surface ready so you can lay them down immediately.

Pat the skin dry rather than rubbing — newborn skin is delicate. Pay particular attention to the skin folds: neck, armpits, behind the knees, groin, and between the fingers and toes. Moisture trapped in folds can lead to irritation or thrush.

If the umbilical cord stump is still present but drying, keep it as dry as possible. Fold the front of the nappy down below the stump to allow air circulation and avoid friction.

## Newborn Skin and Products

Newborn skin is not the same as adult skin. It is thinner, absorbs substances more easily, and its protective acid mantle is still developing. For these reasons, the NHS recommends using plain water for newborn bathing during the first month.

After one month, if you choose to introduce products, look for those labelled as fragrance-free and specifically formulated for newborns. Avoid bubble baths, heavily scented washes, and products containing alcohol. Essential oils — however "natural" — are not appropriate for newborn skin.

Cradle cap, which appears as yellowish crusty patches on the scalp, is extremely common and harmless. Do not scrub or pick at it. A small amount of baby oil applied and left for a short period before bathing can help loosen it gently.

## The Safety Rules That Cannot Be Skipped

Drowning is one of the leading causes of accidental death in children, and it can happen in seconds and in very small amounts of water. The following rules are non-negotiable.

Never leave your baby unattended in the bath, even for one second. If the doorbell rings, if another child calls out, if your phone buzzes — ignore it. Wrap your baby in a towel and take them with you if you must leave the room. There is no legitimate reason to leave an infant alone in water.

Have everything ready before you undress your baby. Once you are committed to the bath, you should not be retrieving items from across the room.

Turn your phone to silent before you start. Even a brief glance at a notification while holding a wet baby over water increases risk.

Bath support seats — the moulded chairs that sit inside a bath or baby bath — are only an additional aid, not a replacement for parental supervision. The NHS and most paediatric safety organisations are explicit: these seats can tip, and a baby sitting in one is not safer than a baby being held. Never treat a bath seat as permission to step away.

Never add hot water while the baby is in the bath.

## After the Bath: Cord Care and Nappy Application

Apply barrier cream to the nappy area before fastening the nappy. Zinc oxide cream provides effective protection against nappy rash. Avoid talcum powder — the NHS advises against it as the fine particles can be inhaled.

Dress your baby in warm layers suitable for the room temperature. Newborns lose heat quickly after bathing, so moving promptly from towel to clothing is important.

If the umbilical cord stump is still healing, keep the area clean and dry, allow air circulation, and fold the nappy below it. Contact your midwife or GP if the area around the stump becomes red, swollen, or smells unpleasant — these are signs of infection.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**When can I bathe my newborn for the first time after birth?**

The NHS recommends waiting until the umbilical cord stump has dried and fallen off, which usually happens between one and three weeks. Before that, top-and-tailing with warm water and cotton wool is the appropriate approach.

**How often should I bathe my newborn?**

Two to three times per week is sufficient. Daily bathing is not necessary and can dry out newborn skin by stripping the natural oils. Top-and-tail daily for the face, neck, hands, and nappy area on non-bath days.

**What temperature should newborn bath water be?**

Between 37 and 38 degrees Celsius — close to body temperature. Test with your elbow or inner wrist rather than your hand. The room should be around 20 to 22 degrees Celsius so your baby stays warm when out of the water.

**Can I use baby bath products from day one?**

The NHS advises using plain water only for the first month. After that, a small amount of mild, fragrance-free baby wash or shampoo is acceptable. Avoid bubble baths, products with fragrance, and anything not specifically formulated for newborns.

**What is the safest way to hold a baby in the bath?**

The cross-cradle hold keeps your non-dominant arm under the baby's back and head throughout the bath, with your hand firmly supporting the back of the head and neck. Your free hand does all the washing. Lower the baby in feet first.

**Is it safe to use a baby bath seat?**

Bath support seats are an additional comfort aid only. They are not a safety device and should never be used as a reason to leave the baby unattended. Both the NHS and paediatric safety organisations are clear that constant parental supervision is required at all times during bathing.

## Key Takeaways

- Wait until the umbilical cord stump has fallen off — usually one to three weeks — before giving a full immersion bath; use top-and-tailing until then.
- Bath water should be 37 to 38 degrees Celsius; test with your elbow or inner wrist, never your hand.
- Two to three baths per week is enough for a newborn; daily bathing dries out developing skin.
- Use the cross-cradle hold throughout the bath, lowering feet first, and keep your supporting arm under the head and neck at all times.
- Never leave a baby unattended in the bath, even for a second — have everything prepared before you start.
- Use plain water for the first month; introduce fragrance-free, mild baby wash after that if you choose.
